cdp.name: CDP
cdp.gloss: Customer Data Platform — the unified store of who your customers are.

perso.name: Personalization (1:1)
perso.gloss: Tailoring the message to the individual, not the segment.

genai.name: Generative AI
genai.gloss: Models that produce content — copy, images, replies — from a prompt.

agentic.name: Agentic AI
agentic.gloss: Systems that don't just generate, they decide and act toward a goal.

decis.name: Agentic decisioning
decis.gloss: An agent choosing what to say, when, how often and on which channel — per person.

bandit.name: Bandits / RL
bandit.gloss: The maths that lets a system learn which choice wins by trying and measuring.

cep.name: CEP
cep.gloss: Customer Engagement Platform — where this messaging actually runs.

geo.name: GEO
geo.gloss: Generative Engine Optimization — being visible inside AI answers.

govern.name: Governance
govern.gloss: The controls that make automated decisions safe, explainable and legible.

stories.head: 91% of Marketers Claim an AI Search Strategy But Most Are Only Scratching the Surface
stories.plain: A survey of 600+ marketing and PR professionals finds a sharp gap between confidence and execution on AI search visibility: 91% say they have a strategy, but 62% already feel behind rivals. This matters because winning placement inside AI-generated answers is fast becoming as important as traditional SEO, yet most teams lack the processes to do it.

stories.head: Looking Beyond the Hype of Agentic AI: How Businesses Can Generate Real ROI
stories.plain: A frank assessment finds that most agentic AI deployments have failed to deliver tangible business benefits and none have achieved enterprise-wide adoption. It lays out what separates costly experiments from genuine ROI, offering practitioners a grounded checklist for evaluating investments — directly relevant to anyone buying or building autonomous marketing systems.

stories.head: Marketers Know What Improves Paid Media ROI but Underinvest in It
stories.plain: New research shows marketers pile budget into AI targeting and creative tools while consistently underfunding post-click experiences they themselves identify as the biggest ROI driver. The finding reveals a structural misalignment in how marketing AI spending is prioritised — with real implications for how teams should allocate technology and content budgets.

stories.head: Look Past AI to See Where MarTech Is Really Going
stories.plain: While AI dominates headlines, the article argues that stack rationalisation, modern measurement, and modular content architectures are the quieter forces reshaping marketing operations. For practitioners buying or governing martech, this is a useful corrective — pointing to where durable structural change is actually happening versus where vendor noise is loudest.

stories.head: Revid.ai Launches MCP Server Giving Autonomous AI Agents Direct Access to Video Production
stories.plain: Revid.ai has opened its video pipeline to autonomous AI agents via the Model Context Protocol, letting agents handle the full script-to-publish workflow without human handoffs. This signals a maturing infrastructure layer where agentic systems can now orchestrate video content at scale — a meaningful shift in how AI-driven content production will be bought and built.

stories.head: 5 questions to ask AI vendors before you buy anything
stories.plain: A practical buyer's guide telling marketers how to cut through vendor hype by probing business value, data policies, and real customer proof before signing. This matters because most teams lack a structured framework for AI procurement, and bad purchases waste budget and create governance risk.

stories.head: 91% of marketers claim an AI search strategy, but most are only scratching the surface
stories.plain: A 600-person survey finds a huge gap between confidence and execution on AI search visibility: 62% say they're already behind competitors. This signals that Generative Engine Optimization is moving from optional experiment to a must-have discipline, and most teams are unprepared.

stories.head: Looking beyond the hype of agentic AI: how businesses can generate real ROI
stories.plain: Analysis finds that the vast majority of agentic AI deployments have failed to deliver tangible results, and no publicly known enterprise-wide rollout exists. Explains why siloed function-level pilots stall and what governance and measurement changes are needed before scaling.

stories.head: Creatio takes aim at the enterprise Frankenstack
stories.plain: Creatio's new platform bundles CRM, AI agents, and workflow automation into one environment to replace the patchwork of disconnected point solutions many enterprises run today. A notable platform-consolidation move that affects how marketing stacks are bought and integrated.

stories.head: Marketers know what improves paid media ROI, but underinvest in it
stories.plain: New research shows marketers pour budget into targeting and AI tools while neglecting post-click experience — the factor they themselves say drives paid media returns. Highlights a structural misalignment between where AI spend goes and where customer value is actually created.

month.skicker: 30-day synthesis · the shape underneath the feed

themes.head: Content is no longer a competitive moat
body: When AI can generate passable content at near-zero cost, volume and polish stop being differentiators. The strategic question shifts from 'how do we produce more?' to 'what can we say that only we can say?' — rooted in proprietary data, lived experience, or genuine point of view.

This coincides with the rise of AI search strategies that 91% of marketers claim to have, yet most are only surface-deep. Brands that treat content as a commodity input will be outflanked by those who treat original insight as the scarce asset worth protecting.
ev: MarTech Series · AI search strategy gap

themes.head: Agentic AI moves from hype to infrastructure
body: Agentic AI is crossing from demo into deployment: Revid.ai's MCP server lets AI agents control video production directly, Casefleet ships real-time agentic document tagging, and platforms are wiring agent-to-agent handoffs into everyday workflows. The infra layer — not the model — is now the battleground.

But ROI is not automatic. Analysts warn that businesses chasing agent hype without clear process ownership will waste budgets. The decisive move is defining which decisions agents can close autonomously versus where a human must stay in the loop — a governance question, not a technology one.
ev: Agile Brand · Agentic ROI reality check

themes.head: The data & stack readiness gap is the real blocker
body: AI adoption has hit 90% at the surface level, yet the underlying data infrastructure most organisations rely on was not built for AI workloads. Fragmented 'Frankenstacks,' inconsistent labelling, and siloed pipelines mean that model quality is capped long before a vendor's algorithm becomes the constraint.

Solutions are converging on consolidation: Creatio targets the enterprise Frankenstack directly, Keylabs.ai evolves enterprise data annotation, and commentators argue that SaaS vendors can no longer compete on software features alone — clean, governed data pipelines are the new product. Marketers who fix the data layer first will outrun those still shopping for better models.

themes.head: Measuring performance not spend is the new mandate
body: Two concurrent findings land hard this month: marketers already know what improves paid media ROI but chronically underinvest in it, and ad spend is routinely mistaken for marketing performance. Confusing activity metrics with outcome metrics is not a data problem — it's a framing problem that AI tooling cannot fix on its own.

The CMS-as-AI-operating-system shift and the rise of autonomous engagement channels make attribution even harder to ignore. Brands that rebuild measurement frameworks around incremental outcomes — not impression counts or budget size — will have the credibility to make smarter reinvestment decisions as agentic spend scales.

themes.head: The human edge & talent question sharpens
body: As AI handles more executional work, two uncomfortable questions surface simultaneously: who trains junior marketers if AI has absorbed the apprenticeship tasks? And how do you retain the institutional judgment that only comes from doing the work? The generational divide compounds this — Gen Z adopts AI fastest yet is also most vocal about its risks.

The answer emerging from practitioners is not to slow AI adoption but to deliberately preserve human decision-making in high-stakes moments — vendor selection, brand positioning, crisis response, customer empathy. Rachel Orston's framing of 'what competitors can't buy' captures the argument: the human edge is a strategic choice, not a technological limitation.
ev: Agile Brand · Gen Z AI divide
Agile Brand · Human edge in automated CX

month.takeaway: Every major move this month — agentic infrastructure, measurement reform, content commoditisation, stack consolidation, talent anxiety — points to the same underlying shift: competitive advantage in MarTech is migrating from what tools you buy to how clearly you can think without them.
